concerns.text: The plan uses DailyDialog (human-human) as a proxy for AI-human interactions to test 'AI Personality Consistency'. The proposed 'Proxy Validity Analysis' (KS test on sentiment distribution) is insufficient to validate this construct. Sentiment distribution similarity does not imply similarity in 'personality' or 'consistency' dynamics. A human-human dataset may have fundamentally different turn-taking, consistency, and trust mechanisms than AI interactions, rendering the core hypothesis untestable with this data regardless of statistical significance.

The 'Lagged Trust' design (FR-009) defines Trust for session N based on turns 1 to N-1, while Consistency for session N is based on turns 2 to N. This creates a mechanical overlap where the 'Trust' outcome (interaction length) is partially derived from the same text used to calculate the 'Consistency' predictor (turns 2 to N-1). This fails to break circularity; the predictor and outcome are not independent, leading to spurious correlation.

The plan proposes Survival Analysis for 'Session Frequency' (days between sessions). Survival analysis requires a defined 'time-to-event' (e.g., time until a user stops interacting). 'Session Frequency' is a rate or interval metric, not a time-to-event metric. Using Cox Proportional Hazards on an interval variable is a methodological mismatch that will produce invalid hazard ratios.

The plan assumes a sample size sufficient to detect r ≥ 0.3 with power ≥ 0.8 but provides no calculation or justification based on the actual DailyDialog subset size. Without a pre-study power analysis or a concrete estimate of the number of valid multi-session users in the dataset, the study risks being underpowered to detect the hypothesized effect, leading to a high Type II error rate.
